perturbation.original_text: 1.2 "Affiliate" with respect to the Village Media Company shall mean any of HOFV and any of the following entities so long as it is directly or indirectly controlled by, or is under common control, with HOFV: HOF Village Stadium, LLC; HOF Village Parking, LLC; HOF Village Land, LLC; HOF Village Youth Fields, LLC; HOF Village Hotel I, LLC; HOF Village Sports Business, LLC; Youth Sports Management, LLC; HOF Village Parking Management I, LLC; HOF Village Residences I, LLC; HOF Village Center for Excellence, LLC; HOF Village Center for Performance, LLC; HOF Experience, LLC; and JCIHOFV Financing, LLC. "Affiliate" with respect to PFHOF shall mean any entity that is directly or indirectly controlled by, or is under common control with, PFHOF. For purposes of this definition, "control" means an equity or income interest of fifty percent (50%) or more, or the possession of the power, directly or indirectly, to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of the Affiliate, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 "Affiliate" with respect to the Village Media Company shall mean any of HOFV and any of the following entities so long as it is directly or indirectly associated with HOFV: HOF Village Stadium, LLC; HOF Village Parking, LLC; HOF Village Land, LLC; HOF Village Youth Fields, LLC; HOF Village Hotel I, LLC; HOF Village Sports Business, LLC; Youth Sports Management, LLC; HOF Village Parking Management I, LLC; HOF Village Residences I, LLC; HOF Village Center for Excellence, LLC; HOF Village Center for Performance, LLC; HOF Experience, LLC; and JCIHOFV Financing, LLC. "Affiliate" with respect to PFHOF shall mean any entity that is directly or indirectly associated with PFHOF. For purposes of this definition, "associated" means having some business dealings.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'controlled by' with 'associated with' and redefining 'control' as 'having some business dealings' broadens the definition of 'Affiliate.' This introduces ambiguity and deviates from the standard legal definition of 'control,' potentially affecting liability and obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition of 'Affiliate' and 'associated' contradicts the SEC's definition of 'control' which emphasizes the power to direct management and policies. The rule focuses on control through ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise. The revised definition introduces ambiguity by using 'associated with' and 'having some business dealings,' which does not meet the SEC's requirement for control.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'controlled by' to 'associated with', and the redefinition of 'control' as 'having some business dealings,' significantly weakens the standard legal definition of control, potentially affecting liability and obligations. The Law Explanation references the SEC's definition of control, emphasizing the power to direct management and policies. This aligns with the perturbation explanation, which states that the revised definition introduces ambiguity. The scraped snippets, while not explicitly defining 'control,' refer to regulations (17 CFR § 240.12b-2) related to definitions within the context of the Securities Exchange Act, implying a more stringent standard of control than 'having some business dealings.' The changed text broadens the definition of affiliate, potentially diluting the legal responsibilities and oversight that come with actual control.

perturbation.original_text: 7.3 Compliance with Laws and Regulations. Each of the Village Media Company and PFHOF, as applicable, agrees to be in material compliance with all federal, state, and local laws, ordinances, and regulations applicable to its respective operations and to obtain and maintain all licenses and permits required by law necessary for each of their respective operations.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.3 Compliance with Laws and Regulations. Each of the Village Media Company and PFHOF, as applicable, agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to comply with all federal, state, and local laws, ordinances, and regulations applicable to its respective operations and to attempt to obtain and maintain all licenses and permits.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'be in material compliance' to 'use commercially reasonable efforts to comply' and 'obtain and maintain' to 'attempt to obtain and maintain' weakens the compliance obligation. This creates uncertainty and might not meet regulatory standards for ensuring compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, replacing 'material compliance' with 'commercially reasonable efforts,' potentially contradicts the Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 404. SOX 404 requires companies to establish and maintain internal controls, which implies more than just 'commercially reasonable efforts'. The original text ensures a commitment to 'material compliance,' but the modified version weakens this assurance, potentially failing to meet the SOX requirement for effective internal controls.

Management assessment of internal controls (a) Rules required The Commission shall prescribe rules requiring each annual report required by section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title to contain an internal control report, which shall- (1) state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control stru
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a legal contradiction by weakening the compliance obligation. The original text requires 'material compliance,' while the changed text only requires 'commercially reasonable efforts to comply.' The LLM Law Explanation suggests that this contradicts Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 404, which requires companies to establish and maintain internal controls, implying more than just 'commercially reasonable efforts.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 7262, discusses the management assessment of internal controls, supporting the idea that companies are required to actively maintain internal controls to ensure compliance. The change to 'commercially reasonable efforts' undermines this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: 6. FORCE MAJEURE In the event either Party is unable to comply fully with its obligations (other than payment obligations) under this Agreement due to an event beyond the control of the Party whose performance is affected, including any legal prohibition, court order, degree, regulation or requirement of any governmental entity having jurisdiction, strikes, catastrophe, drought, shortage of water or other action of the elements, temporary or permanent shutdown due to regulatory or other governmental actions, or Acts of God or any other matters beyond its control, such Party shall while so affected be relieved to the extent thus prevented from performing its obligations hereunder and such non-performance shall not, in and of itself, be deemed to be a breach of this Agreement; provided that, in such event, the non-performing Party takes all commercially reasonable measures to remove the disability and resume full performance hereunder at the earliest possible date.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. FORCE MAJEURE In the event either Party is unable to comply fully with its obligations (other than payment obligations) under this Agreement due to an event beyond the control of the Party whose performance is affected, including strikes, catastrophe, drought, shortage of water or other action of the elements, or Acts of God or any other matters beyond its control, such Party shall while so affected be relieved to the extent thus prevented from performing its obligations hereunder and such non-performance shall not, in and of itself, be deemed to be a breach of this Agreement; provided that, in such event, the non-performing Party takes all commercially reasonable measures to remove the disability and resume full performance hereunder at the earliest possible date.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'any legal prohibition, court order, degree, regulation or requirement of any governmental entity having jurisdiction, temporary or permanent shutdown due to regulatory or other governmental actions' from the definition of Force Majeure, the contract limits excusable non-performance. This contradicts the standard legal understanding of force majeure.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing 'legal prohibition, court order, decree, regulation' from the force majeure clause contradicts UCC 2-615, which addresses situations where performance becomes impracticable due to unforeseen circumstances, including government regulations. The original clause aligns with the intent of UCC 2-615 by acknowledging legal and regulatory impediments as potential force majeure events. Eliminating these elements narrows the scope of force majeure protection, deviating from the UCC’s provisions for excusable non-performance due to governmental actions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. Except so far as a seller may have assumed a greater obligation and subject to the preceding section on substituted performance: (a) Delay in delivery or non-delivery in whole or in part by a seller who complies with paragraphs (b) and (c) is not a breach of his duty under a contract for sale if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made or by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order whether or not it later proves to be invalid. (b) Where the causes mentioned in paragraph (a) affect only a part of the seller's capacity to perform, he must allocate production and deliveries among his customers but may at his option include regular customers not then under contract as well as his own requirements for further manufacture. He may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable. (c) The seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will be delay or non-delivery and, when allocation is required under paragraph (b), of the estimated quota thus made available for the buyer. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes specific instances that would trigger force majeure, namely, 'any legal prohibition, court order, degree, regulation or requirement of any governmental entity having jurisdiction, temporary or permanent shutdown due to regulatory or other governmental actions,' thereby narrowing the scope of events excusing non-performance. The law snippet from UCC 2-615 states that non-delivery is not a breach if performance is made impracticable by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order. By omitting these governmental regulations from the force majeure clause, the contract contradicts the UCC's provision for excusable non-performance due to governmental actions.
